title: Web scraping, step by step
text: This walks through getting data off a website that doesn’t hand it to you nicely — using the exact process behind the Uso del tiempo page, including the dead ends.

title: Web scraping, step by step
section: Step 1: Look for the easy path first
text:
Step 1: Look for the easy path first
Before writing a single line of scraping code, check whether the data is already published somewhere structured:

A national/organizational open data portal (in Chile: datos.gob.cl). Many of these run CKAN, which exposes a clean JSON API — no HTML parsing needed:

curl "https://datos.gob.cl/api/3/action/package_show?id=<dataset-name>"

A “download” or “bases de datos” section on the source site itself.







WarningDead end #1



For ENUT, the CKAN entry on datos.gob.cl existed but had zero attached files — just a description pointing back to INE’s own site. Open data portals are sometimes just metadata catalogs, not actual mirrors. Worth checking, but don’t assume it’ll have the file.

title: Web scraping, step by step
section: Step 2: Fetch the page and see what’s actually there
text:
Step 2: Fetch the page and see what’s actually there

Bash (curl)Python (requests)R (rvest)


curl -sL -A "Mozilla/5.0" "https://example.com/page" -o page.html
grep -oE 'href="[^"]*\.(csv|xlsx|zip)"' page.html


import requests
r = requests.get("https://example.com/page", headers={"User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0"})
r.raise_for_status()
html = r.text


library(rvest)
page <- read_html("https://example.com/page")
page |> html_elements("a") |> html_attr("href")



Always set a User-Agent — some servers block requests that don’t look like a browser. Always follow redirects (-L in curl).






WarningDead end #2



Fetching INE’s ENUT page this way returned real HTML, but grepping it for .zip/.xlsx/.csv links came back empty. The only file links in the raw HTML were three PDFs. The “Bases de Datos” panel visible in the browser simply wasn’t in the server-rendered page.

title: Web scraping, step by step
section: Step 3: Figure out why the data isn’t in the HTML
text:
Step 3: Figure out why the data isn’t in the HTML
If a link is visible in your browser but missing from curl’s output, the page is probably building that part of the DOM with JavaScript after load — an AJAX call, a client-side framework, or an accordion that only populates on click. curl never runs JavaScript, so it only ever sees the initial server response.
How to confirm it and find the real source:

Open the page in a real browser.
Open DevTools → Network tab, reload, and watch for XHR/fetch requests — the data is often coming from a JSON or file endpoint you can hit directly.
If it’s a static link that’s just injected by JS (our case), hover over the link and read the URL in the status bar, or right-click → “Copy link address.”







TipWhat actually worked here



Hovering over “Base de datos R II ENUT 2023” in the browser revealed the real URL in the status bar: https://www.ine.gob.cl/docs/default-source/uso-del-tiempo-tiempo-libre/bbdd/ii-enut/250403-ii-enut-bdd-r-v2.zip No JavaScript execution needed on my end — once I had the direct URL, a plain curl download worked fine. Sometimes the fastest fix for “the scraper can’t find it” is a human spending 10 seconds in the browser.


If there’s truly no static URL to grab (the content is generated entirely by client-side JS with no discoverable endpoint), the fallback is a headless browser like Playwright or Selenium, which actually renders the page before you extract from it. That’s slower and heavier — reach for it only after Network-tab inspection comes up empty.

title: Web scraping, step by step
text:
Step 4: Download and unpack
curl -sL -A "Mozilla/5.0" "<direct-file-url>" -o data.zip
unzip -o data.zip -d data/

title: Web scraping, step by step
section: Step 5: Parse whatever format you land on
text:
Step 5: Parse whatever format you land on
Not everything is a friendly CSV. This dataset came as an .RDS — R’s native serialization format — but Python can read it without installing R, via pyreadr:

Python (no R needed)R (native)


import pyreadr
result = pyreadr.read_r("data.RDS")
df = list(result.values())[0]   # RDS holding one object -> one entry
print(df.shape, list(df.columns)[:20])


df <- readRDS("data.RDS")
str(df)



Other common formats and their tools:



Format
Python
R




CSV
pandas.read_csv
readr::read_csv


Excel
pandas.read_excel
readxl::read_excel


SPSS .sav
pyreadstat
haven::read_sav


Stata .dta
pandas.read_stata
haven::read_dta


PDF tables
pdfplumber / camelot
tabulizer

title: Web scraping, step by step
section: Step 6: Understand the codebook before trusting the numbers
text:
Step 6: Understand the codebook before trusting the numbers
Raw survey data is full of cryptic column names (t_tnr_ds, fe_cut, varstrat…). Before charting anything:

Find the methodology document or variable dictionary — it explains naming conventions, units, and which weight/expansion-factor column to use for population-representative estimates.
Validate against a number the publisher already reported (a headline stat from their own press release or summary report). If your computed number is in the right ballpark, your variable/weight choices are probably correct.







Tip



For ENUT, INE’s own methodology PDF explained the naming scheme: prefixes like tnr (trabajo no remunerado), to (trabajo remunerado), suffixes _dt/_fds/_ds (weekday/weekend/weekly-average), and that estimates should be weighted by fe_cut. Computing the gender gap in unpaid work with that weight landed close to INE’s own published figure — a good sign the approach was right.

title: Web scraping, step by step
section: Step 7: Be a good citizen with what you publish
text:
Step 7: Be a good citizen with what you publish

Don’t commit large raw third-party files to a git repo — store the small, derived/aggregated results you actually need (a few KB of JSON), and document how to regenerate them from the source.
Cite the source and its license/date. Data changes; someone reading your chart in two years should be able to find the original.
If a site has an API, use it instead of scraping next time — friendlier to them and more stable for you.

title: Web scraping, step by step
text:
Checklist

Checked robots.txt and the site’s terms/license
Looked for an existing open-data/API entry first
Fetched the raw HTML and confirmed whether the data is actually there
If missing: checked DevTools Network tab / hovered links for the real URL
Downloaded once, cached locally, didn’t hammer the server
Found and read the codebook/methodology before computing anything
Sanity-checked results against a published figure
Committed only small derived outputs, not the raw dump — with source + license noted

title: Showcase
text: This page is a grab-bag of Quarto features, rendered right in your site. Open the .qmd source alongside this page to see how each piece is written.

title: Showcase
text:
Callouts
Callouts are a nice way to flag notes, tips, warnings, or important info without writing any HTML.






Note



This is a note. Good for asides and extra context.








TipPro tip



Callouts can have their own titles, like this one.








Warning



This is a warning. Use it to flag things readers should be careful about.








Important



This is important. Reserve it for things that really matter.








NoteClick to expand





Callouts can also be collapsible — handy for optional detail or spoilers.

title: Showcase
text:
Tabsets
Tabsets let you show alternative content (e.g. different languages or OS instructions) without cluttering the page.

macOSWindowsLinux


brew install quarto


winget install --id Posit.Quarto


sudo apt install quarto

title: Showcase
text:
Columns / layout
Quarto uses a grid system so you can lay content out side by side.



Left column
Put anything here — text, images, code.



Right column
This is great for before/after comparisons or a figure next to its explanation.

title: Showcase
text:
Code blocks
Plain fenced code blocks get syntax highlighting for free, with optional line numbers:
def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("world"))
You can also fold long code blocks so they don’t dominate the page — click to expand:
import math

def area_of_circle(radius: float) -> float:
    return math.pi * radius ** 2

for r in range(1, 5):
    print(f"radius={r} -> area={area_of_circle(r):.2f}")

title: Showcase
Showcase
text:
Tables








Feature
Needs a kernel?
Good for




Callouts
No
Notes, warnings, tips


Tabsets
No
Alternatives (OS, language)


Observable JS
No
Live interactive charts/widgets


Python/R code chunks
Yes (Jupyter/knitr)
Data analysis, real computation

title: Showcase
text:
Footnotes & cross-references
You can drop a footnote right in the text.1 Figures and tables can also be numbered and cross-referenced automatically once you add captions and labels.

title: Showcase
text:
Theme toggle
Look at the navbar — there’s now a light/dark switcher (configured in _quarto.yml via theme: {light: cosmo, dark: darkly}). Try it.

title: Showcase
text:
Where to go next

Callouts
Tabsets
Observable JS
Themes
Full website guide

title: Showcase
text:
Footnotes


Like this one — Quarto collects them at the bottom of the page automatically.↩︎

title: Build a site like this one
text: This walks through everything needed to go from nothing to a live Quarto website on GitHub Pages, the same way this site (kokoromi) is set up. Commands are shown for the shell (bash/PowerShell); where R users have a native alternative (e.g. via the quarto and usethis packages), that’s shown in its own tab.

title: Build a site like this one
text:
1. Install the prerequisites

macOSWindowsLinux


brew install --cask quarto
brew install git gh          # gh = GitHub CLI, optional but handy


winget install --id Posit.Quarto
winget install --id Git.Git
winget install --id GitHub.cli


sudo apt install git
# Quarto: download the .deb from https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
sudo dpkg -i quarto-*.deb



Confirm everything is on your PATH:
quarto --version
git --version






Tip



If you plan to write posts that execute R code, also install R and, inside R, install.packages("rmarkdown"). For Python code chunks, you need jupyter (pip install jupyter). Neither is required for plain Markdown pages or Observable JS demos — see the Showcase page.

title: Build a site like this one
text:
2. Create the GitHub repo

Bash (gh CLI)R (usethis)


gh repo create <your-username>.github.io --public --clone
cd <your-username>.github.io


usethis::create_github_repo(
  "<your-username>.github.io",
  private = FALSE
)



If you’d rather not use a CLI, create the repo in the GitHub web UI, then clone it:
git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-username>.github.io.git
cd <your-username>.github.io

title: Build a site like this one
text:
3. Scaffold a Quarto website project
Inside the repo folder:
quarto create project website .
This generates _quarto.yml, an index.qmd, an about.qmd, and a starter styles.css. Answer the prompts (project title, etc.) — you can always change them later.






TipR alternative



From the R console, the quarto package wraps the same CLI:
quarto::quarto_create_project(name = ".", type = "website")

title: Build a site like this one
section: 4. Point the build output at docs/
text:
4. Point the build output at docs/
GitHub Pages needs a specific folder to serve from. Rather than fiddling with branches, the simplest option is: render straight into docs/ and tell GitHub Pages to serve that folder. In _quarto.yml:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs

title: Build a site like this one
text:
5. Configure navigation
Still in _quarto.yml, set up a navbar and/or a docked sidebar — only listing pages that actually exist:
website:
  title: "your-site-name"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About
  sidebar:
    style: "docked"
    search: true
    contents:
      - index.qmd
      - about.qmd

format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: darkly   # gives you a free light/dark toggle
    css: styles.css






Warning



Every file listed under navbar or sidebar must exist as an actual .qmd in the repo. A stale reference (say, from a template you copy-pasted) won’t error loudly — it’ll just render as a dead link, and it’s easy to miss until someone clicks it.

title: Build a site like this one
text:
6. Write your pages
Each page is a .qmd file with a YAML header and Markdown/code below:
---
title: "About"
---

Some content here.
Add as many as you like, and list them in _quarto.yml so they show up in navigation.

title: Build a site like this one
text:
7. Set up .gitignore correctly
Quarto’s local working directory (_site/ if you render outside the project, .quarto/ cache) shouldn’t be committed — but your output (docs/) must be:
/.quarto/
_site/
**/*.quarto_ipynb






WarningA real pitfall



Don’t ever paste git status output into .gitignore — it’s an easy copy/paste slip, and the result silently excludes real site files (like index.html or newly generated CSS) from every future commit. The symptom is subtle: your site renders fine locally but looks broken/unstyled once pushed, because new asset files never made it into the repo.

title: Build a site like this one
section: 8. Render and preview locally — before pushing
text:
8. Render and preview locally — before pushing
quarto render        # full project render (not `quarto render index.qmd`!)
quarto preview        # opens a live-reloading local server






Important



Always render the whole project (quarto render with no arguments), not a single file. Rendering one .qmd directly skips the site-wide navbar/sidebar/theme wiring, and that page will come out looking unstyled compared to the rest of the site.

title: Build a site like this one
text:
9. Commit and push
git add .
git commit -m "Initial site"
git push origin main






TipR alternative



quarto::quarto_render()
gert::git_add(".")
gert::git_commit("Initial site")
gert::git_push()

title: Build a site like this one
text:
10. Turn on GitHub Pages
In the GitHub web UI:

Go to your repo → Settings → Pages.
Under Build and deployment, set Source to Deploy from a branch.
Set Branch to main and the folder to /docs.
Save, then wait a minute or two.

Your site will be live at https://<your-username>.github.io/.






NoteAlternative: GitHub Actions



Instead of committing rendered HTML at all, you can let GitHub build it for you on every push using the quarto-actions workflow, and deploy from a gh-pages branch instead of docs/. That keeps your main branch free of generated files — more setup up front, but a cleaner history long-term.

title: Build a site like this one
text:
Checklist

Quarto CLI + Git installed
Repo named <username>.github.io
_quarto.yml has output-dir: docs
Navbar/sidebar only reference pages that exist
.gitignore excludes _site/ and .quarto/, not docs/
quarto render run on the full project, no errors/warnings
Pushed to main
GitHub Pages source set to main / docs

title: Build a site like this one
text:
Where to go next

Quarto websites guide
Showcase page — callouts, tabsets, live demos
GitHub Pages docs

title: About
text: About this site

title: kokoromi
text:
This is a Quarto website.
To learn more about Quarto websites visit https://quarto.org/docs/websites.

title: Uso del tiempo en Chile
text:
NoteFuente de los datos



Instituto Nacional de Estadísticas (INE) de Chile, II Encuesta Nacional sobre Uso del Tiempo (II ENUT) 2023 — 48.020 personas encuestadas en centros urbanos de las 16 regiones del país. Base de datos descargada desde ine.gob.cl/enut (formato R, licencia CC BY-NC). Los promedios de esta página se calculan ponderando por el factor de expansión de personas del módulo de uso del tiempo (fe_cut); las celdas con menos de 30 casos muestrales se omiten.

title: Uso del tiempo en Chile
section: El balance diario de tiempo, por sexo
text:
El balance diario de tiempo, por sexo
En promedio, ¿cuántas horas al día destina cada persona a trabajo remunerado, trabajo no remunerado, cuidado personal, educación y ocio?

balance = FileAttachment("data/enut/time_balance_by_sex.json").json()







Plot.plot({
  marginLeft: 160,
  x: {label: "Horas por día →"},
  y: {label: null},
  color: {legend: true, domain: ["Hombres", "Mujeres"], range: ["#5c9e95", "#c7a3c0"]},
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(balance, {
      y: "domain",
      x: "hours",
      fy: "domain",
      fill: "sex",
      tip: true
    }),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ],
  fy: {label: null, tickFormat: () => ""},
  height: 320
})












Tip



Los hombres destinan más horas al día al trabajo remunerado; las mujeres destinan considerablemente más al trabajo no remunerado. Esa es, en gran parte, la “doble jornada”: cuando se suman ambos tipos de trabajo, la carga total de trabajo de las mujeres es mayor.

title: Uso del tiempo en Chile
section: ¿En qué consiste ese trabajo no remunerado?
text:
¿En qué consiste ese trabajo no remunerado?
El trabajo no remunerado agrupa tres grandes categorías: cuidados a otras personas del hogar, trabajo doméstico, y trabajo voluntario o ayuda a otros hogares.

breakdown = FileAttachment("data/enut/unpaid_breakdown_by_sex.json").json()







Plot.plot({
  marginLeft: 220,
  x: {label: "Horas por día →"},
  y: {label: null},
  color: {legend: true, domain: ["Hombres", "Mujeres"], range: ["#5c9e95", "#c7a3c0"]},
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(breakdown, {
      y: "category",
      x: "hours",
      fill: "sex",
      tip: true
    }),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ],
  height: 260
})

title: Uso del tiempo en Chile
section: El cuidado también está segregado en el mercado laboral
text:
El cuidado también está segregado en el mercado laboral
Hasta aquí, todo el trabajo de cuidados visto era no remunerado. Pero el cuidado también existe como empleo: salud, educación y cuidado personal remunerados. ¿Qué proporción de las personas ocupadas trabaja en cuidados remunerados, y cómo se distribuye por sexo?






NoteFuente distinta a los gráficos anteriores



Este gráfico usa una clasificación por ocupación (CIUO-08, 2 dígitos) y sector (CAENES), que no está en la base de datos pública de la ENUT — solo en un archivo interno de procesamiento del INE con mayor nivel de detalle. Solo las proporciones agregadas y sus intervalos de confianza salieron de ese archivo; ningún microdato se publica aquí. Se calcularon con diseño muestral complejo (svydesign + svyby, con estratos y conglomerados) usando scripts/build_paid_care_workforce.R. Se muestran tres definiciones, de más estricta a más amplia: núcleo (ocupación y sector de cuidados a la vez), ampliada (incluye facilitadores del cuidado) y máxima (incluye además habilitadores).



paidCare = FileAttachment("data/enut/paid_care_workforce_by_sex.json").json()







Plot.plot({
  marginLeft: 160,
  x: {label: "Proporción de personas ocupadas →", percent: true, domain: [0, 0.4]},
  y: {label: null},
  fy: {label: null},
  color: {legend: true, domain: ["Hombres", "Mujeres"], range: ["#5c9e95", "#c7a3c0"]},
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(paidCare, {y: "sex", x: "share", fy: "definition", fill: "sex", tip: true}),
    Plot.ruleX(paidCare, {y: "sex", fy: "definition", x1: "ci_low", x2: "ci_high", stroke: "currentColor", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ],
  height: 340
})












Important



Bajo la definición núcleo, las mujeres ocupadas tienen más de 3 veces la probabilidad de trabajar en cuidados remunerados que los hombres — y los intervalos de confianza de ambos sexos no se superponen en ninguna de las tres definiciones. La segregación de género en el cuidado no es solo dentro del hogar: se repite en el mercado laboral.

title: Uso del tiempo en Chile
section: La brecha a lo largo de la vida
text:
La brecha a lo largo de la vida
¿La brecha de género en trabajo no remunerado es constante, o cambia según la edad?

byAge = FileAttachment("data/enut/unpaid_by_age_sex.json").json()







Plot.plot({
  x: {label: "Grupo etario"},
  y: {label: "Horas por día en trabajo no remunerado →"},
  color: {legend: true, domain: ["Hombres", "Mujeres"], range: ["#5c9e95", "#c7a3c0"]},
  marks: [
    Plot.lineY(byAge, {x: "age_group", y: "hours", stroke: "sex", marker: "circle", tip: true}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ],
  height: 320
})












Important



La brecha se abre fuerte entre los 18-24 y los 35-44 años — los años típicos de crianza — y se mantiene amplia el resto de la vida, incluso después de los 65.

title: Uso del tiempo en Chile
text:
La brecha por región

byRegion = FileAttachment("data/enut/unpaid_gap_by_region.json").json()







Plot.plot({
  marginLeft: 260,
  x: {label: "Brecha Mujeres − Hombres (horas/día) →"},
  y: {label: null, domain: byRegion.map(d => d.region)},
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(byRegion, {y: "region", x: "gap", fill: "#c7a3c0", tip: true, sort: {y: "x", reverse: true}}),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ],
  height: 420
})

title: Uso del tiempo en Chile
text:
Cómo se hizo esta página

Se descargó la base de datos en formato R (.RDS) desde la sección Bases de Datos de ine.gob.cl/enut.
Se procesó con scripts/build_enut_data.py (Python: pyreadr + pandas) para calcular promedios ponderados por sexo, categoría de actividad, edad y región. Hay también una versión en R: scripts/build_enut_data.R.
Los resultados agregados se guardaron como archivos .json pequeños (no la base completa) en data/enut/.
Esta página los carga con Observable JS y los grafica con Observable Plot — todo se ejecuta en el navegador, sin backend.
El gráfico de cuidado remunerado usa una fuente distinta: un archivo interno de procesamiento del INE con clasificación detallada de ocupación, procesado con scripts/build_paid_care_workforce.R usando diseño muestral complejo (survey::svydesign). Solo las proporciones agregadas salieron de ese archivo.

Quieres aprender a hacer esto con tus propios datos? Mira la página de web scraping.
